title: Dataclasses vs Pydantic

glossary.definition: Automatically converting a value from one data type to another, like turning the string 5 into the integer 5.
The extra computing power or time required to run a specific feature, like data validation.

overview.what: Standard library `@dataclass` generates boilerplate methods (like `__init__`, `__repr__`) for storing structured data, but it does not strictly enforce types at runtime. Pydantic's `BaseModel` does data parsing, type coercion, and strict validation. Mental model: Dataclasses are lightweight 'storage boxes' trusting the developer to provide correct types; Pydantic models are 'bouncers' that actively inspect, cast, and reject invalid data coming from the outside world.
overview.why: Choosing the right tool prevents performance overhead when validation isn't needed, and prevents bugs when dealing with untrusted I/O data (like JSON from a web request).

why_learning_this: You will know when to pay the performance cost of Pydantic validation.
You will avoid the false sense of security that standard dataclasses type hints give.

common_mistakes.title: Assuming dataclasses enforce types
common_mistakes.explanation: If you define `age: int` in a dataclass, passing `age='twenty'` will silently succeed at runtime. Dataclasses only store what they are given unless checked by a static type checker like mypy.

common_mistakes.title: Using Pydantic everywhere
common_mistakes.explanation: Using Pydantic for purely internal data structures (where you completely control the inputs) adds unnecessary CPU overhead due to its validation checks on every instantiation.

recall_questions.q: What happens if you pass a string to an `int` field in a standard Python `@dataclass`?
recall_questions.answer: It silently stores the string. Standard dataclasses do not enforce or cast types at runtime.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary feature Pydantic provides that standard dataclasses do not?
recall_questions.answer: Runtime data parsing, type coercion, and strict validation of inputs.

recall_questions.q: When is it better to use a standard `@dataclass` over a Pydantic `BaseModel`?
recall_questions.answer: When storing internal state or trusted data where validation is unnecessary and performance/minimal overhead is desired.

understanding_checks.question: What is printed when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.why: Standard dataclasses do not perform type validation or coercion at runtime. The string `'123'` is stored exactly as passed, even though the type hint is `int`.

understanding_checks.question: If you receive a JSON payload from an external API, which library should you use to load it into a Python object, and why?
understanding_checks.why: Because external APIs are untrusted I/O. Pydantic will validate the types and coerce data (e.g., parsing ISO datetime strings into datetime objects) or raise a validation error, whereas a dataclass will blindly accept bad data.

code_walkthrough.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Settings(BaseModel):
    port: int

s = Settings(port='8080')
print(repr(s.port))
code_walkthrough.focus: s = Settings(port='8080') causes Pydantic to parse the string '8080' and coerce it into the integer 8080.

aha_moment.prediction: Will this code crash with a standard Python TypeError or print a message?
aha_moment.common_guess: It will crash with a TypeError.
aha_moment.why: It prints 'Validation Failed!'. Unlike standard python types or dataclasses, Pydantic actively intercepts the instantiation, catches the type mismatch, and raises its own rich `ValidationError`.

practice_tasks.title: Upgrade to Validated Models
practice_tasks.prompt: Given this standard dataclass that blindly accepts bad data, modify it to be a Pydantic `BaseModel` so that it coerces types and validates inputs.

challenge.title: Choosing the Right Tool
challenge.prompt: Create two classes: an internal, unvalidated `InternalState` using `@dataclass` (with field `status: str`), and an external, validated `Payload` using Pydantic's `BaseModel` (with field `amount: float`).
